b' Zucker\xe2\x80\x99s Bid, Kara\xe2\x80\x99s Baby & Zaz Deal Clues
Greetings from a comfortable cruising altitude, where I\xe2\x80\x99m still recovering from Puck\xe2\x80\x99s delightful third-anniversary party, a capstone to our multiday on-site off-site in New York. In tonight\xe2\x80\x99s email, news and notes on Zaz\xe2\x80\x99s Charter deal, and what it does and doesn\xe2\x80\x99t portend for future negotiations with Brian Roberts. Plus, more on the Max-Paramount+ bundle. Greetings from a comfortable cruising altitude, where I\xe2\x80\x99m still recovering from Puck\xe2\x80\x99s delightful third-anniversary party, a capstone to our multiday on- site off-site in New York. It was wonderful to see so many of you on the rooftop at Nine Orchard last night and to take stock of just how far we\xe2\x80\x99ve come since our early days at 64 Bank Street.

Also, a very sincere thank-you for all the leads and tips you shared, which have given me a lot to chase down in the days ahead. And thanks to whoever picked up the tab at the afterparty (wasn\xe2\x80\x99t me). Anyway, it\xe2\x80\x99s been a helluva week. I\xe2\x80\x99m en route back to Los Angeles. If you\xe2\x80\x99re headed west for the Emmys, I look forward to seeing you at baggage claim.

In tonight\xe2\x80\x99s email, news and notes on Zaz \xe2\x80\x99s Charter deal, and what it does and doesn\xe2\x80\x99t portend for future negotiations with Brian Roberts . Plus, more on the Max-Paramount+ bundle.

But first\xe2\x80\xa6

  • Zucker\xe2\x80\x99s game : Jeff Zucker \xe2\x80\x99s RedBird IMI recently succeeded in offloading The Spectator to hedge fund chief Paul Marshall for an eye-popping \xc2\xa3100 million, and will accept final bids for The Telegraph in just two weeks. As you\xe2\x80\x99ll recall, due to foreign ownership restrictions, the U.K. government forced Zucker to sell both the magazine and the paper shortly after he\xe2\x80\x99d acquired them.

    It\xe2\x80\x99s unclear what The Telegraph will fetch: I\xe2\x80\x99m told that subscription and revenue growth has slowed at the Tory broadsheet since Zucker first bought the asset. Meanwhile, the auction has been going on so long that the initial euphoria may have faded. Oh/and: The anti-Zucker legislation that capped foreign sovereign ownership at 5 percent has limited the available pool of prospective buyers.

    So, who\xe2\x80\x99s still in the mix? Three potential buyers: Marshall and Ken Griffin certainly have the money, and might like to pair The Telegraph with The Spectator . Former Mirror C.E.O. David Montgomery is also said to be in the swirl, though staff fear that he would be a ruthless cost-cutter (he is apparently referred to as \xe2\x80\x9cthe dour Ulsterman\xe2\x80\x9d). And then there\xe2\x80\x99s New York Sun publisher Dovid Efune , who now has backing from former Telegraph proprietor Conrad Black . On a related note, Rupert Murdoch is said to be interested in acquiring something , but I\xe2\x80\x99m reliably told it won\xe2\x80\x99t be The Telegraph.

  • End of Code : Vox Media is suspending its Code Conference, the annual two-day tech summit created by Kara Swisher and Walt Mossberg . After more than 20 years of memorable, news-making interviews with tech luminaries\xe2\x80\x94 Steve Jobs , the sweating Mark Zuckerberg , etcetera\xe2\x80\x94Kara took on an \xc3\xa9minence grise role last year and passed the reins to Nilay Patel , Casey Newton , and Julia Boorstin . Already, Vox seems to have concluded that the new iteration isn\xe2\x80\x99t worth the investment. Code, after all, was Kara\xe2\x80\x99s thing. Now, conferences have become the Zyns of the media business\xe2\x80\x94addictive, sure, but high cost, low margin, and event production by another name. It was wise of C.E.O. Jim Bankoff to sunset this thing , even if many of us will miss the after-hours poker game that Brooke Hammerling operated inside the Beverly Hilton every year.

And now to the main event\xe2\x80\xa6

This Too Shall Zaz
David Zaslav may be trumpeting a face-saving deal with Charter, but the forthcoming face-off with Comcast isn\xe2\x80\x99t going to be any easier.
DYLAN BYERS
On Thursday, just before his appearance at a Goldman Sachs investor conference, David Zaslav announced that Warner Bros. Discovery had struck a new distribution agreement with Charter that will increase the overall fees the distributor pays to air CNN, TNT, and WBD\xe2\x80\x99s other TV networks. The deal, which Zaz closed ahead of schedule, gave him a lot to crow about at the conference and, more importantly, sent his beleaguered stock soaring 10 percent\xe2\x80\x94its best one-day performance in nearly a year.

Of course, the deal was significant because it came on the heels of WBD forfeiting its NBA rights after a mismanaged negotiation and absorbing a staggering $9.1 billion write-down on the value of its cable networks. Skeptical analysts have correctly warned that Zaz could get screwed in the rights renewal negotiations without the NBA\xe2\x80\x94in fact, according to the work of my partner John Ourand , Comcast was motivated to overpay for league rights partly to do just that . And, to be sure, the fees for a neutered TNT, itself, remained flat at roughly $3 per sub in the Charter deal. But Zaz was nevertheless able to tout a \xe2\x80\x9cterrific deal\xe2\x80\x9d overall. Meanwhile, The Wall Street Journal claimed the deal would \xe2\x80\x9clay the foundation\xe2\x80\x9d for WBD\xe2\x80\x99s negotiations with other providers, and specifically Comcast.

That\xe2\x80\x99s great press, but the Charter deal has almost no bearing on Zaz\xe2\x80\x99s future negotiations with Comcast, as well-placed Comcast sources confirmed to me this week. Reports on the Charter deal omit a key relevant detail: WBD board member and Zaz sensei John Malone \xe2\x80\x99s Liberty Media also owns a significant, 26 percent stake in Charter. Malone isn\xe2\x80\x99t putting his finger on the scale , obviously, but he\xe2\x80\x99s effectively setting the market for himself. \xe2\x80\x9cLiberty wasn\xe2\x80\x99t going to undermine WBD through Charter\xe2\x80\x9d any more than Brian Roberts would let Comcast hose NBCUniversal, one veteran media executive noted. Meanwhile, this source added, \xe2\x80\x9cComcast is not going to care one iota about a Charter deal with WBD.\xe2\x80\x9d

Indeed, as another source told me back in early August, Roberts and Comcast are likely to \xe2\x80\x9cput the screws\xe2\x80\x9d to WBD during their next rights negotiations. In retrospect, Roberts\xe2\x80\x99 aggressive play for Zaz\xe2\x80\x99s NBA rights seems to have been part of a brilliant and multifaceted corporate chess maneuver: By submitting a bid that Zaz and his debt-saddled company couldn\xe2\x80\x99t match, Roberts won coveted sports rights for NBC and deprived his competitor of the asset that commands hefty subscriber fees. And no, March Madness, MLB, NASCAR, and a smattering of other assets aren\xe2\x80\x99t going to fill that void (still, congrats to Zaz for getting the French Open, another excuse to return to the Crillon). As one Comcast source told me this week, the Charter deal \xe2\x80\x9cdoesn\xe2\x80\x99t lay a foundation. It doesn\xe2\x80\x99t mean anything .\xe2\x80\x9d

On a related note, some industry insiders believe that Roberts might also leverage CNN\xe2\x80\x99s low ratings to keep fees down during the negotiations. That might seem like a moderate act of war, but it\xe2\x80\x99s not entirely unfounded. Outside of a few major political events, including its presidential debate and Kamala Harris interview, CNN has been putting up some of the worst ratings in its history. During Tuesday\xe2\x80\x99s debate, CNN drew just over 4 million viewers, coming in sixth place behind all the other major broadcast and cable news networks, even though the network ostensibly transported its entire stable of primetime broadcasters and anchors down to Philadelphia. Notably, it marked the first time in the history of presidential debates that MSNBC outperformed CNN in total viewers.
The Zaz Bundle Love Language
On the streaming front, Zaz said that Max was poised to add 6 million subscribers and touted its forthcoming tie-up with Disney+ and Hulu. Of course, the Hulu piece is also complicated by Comcast, which remains in a stalemate with Disney over relinquishing its one-third ownership position in that joint venture. (My partner Bill Cohan will have some more insight into Zaz\xe2\x80\x99s streaming promise to Wall Street in Dry Powder on Sunday. Sign up here .)

On a tangential note, that delay has also placed Disney in a strange holding pattern: Entertainment co-chairs Dana Walden and Alan Bergman are eager to update Hulu\xe2\x80\x99s backend tech, which hasn\xe2\x80\x99t been meaningfully enhanced in years. They also want to grow engagement\xe2\x80\x94specifically, the number of hours watched by subscribers to the ad-supported tier (the internal shorthand is SASH, for \xe2\x80\x9csubscriber ad-supported hours\xe2\x80\x9d)\xe2\x80\x94and focus on the integration. But they can\xe2\x80\x99t move forward until Comcast relinquishes control.

Meanwhile, Zaz is also hinting at more bundles down the line, the most obvious of which would be a partnership with the soon-to-be Skydance-owned Paramount+. As I reported earlier this week , Zaz and Paramount interim co-C.E.O. George Cheeks have been discussing bundling their two streaming services. Cheeks is leading those talks because Skydance\xe2\x80\x99s David Ellison and his Paramount Global president-to-be Jeff Shell aren\xe2\x80\x99t allowed to participate in such negotiations until the deal closes. Of course, Cheeks is talking to other potential partners as well, pitching them on the increased value of Paramount\xe2\x80\x99s I.P. library once it includes Skydance titles.

Nearly two and a half years into the WBD journey, Zaz may be learning a gut-churning lesson or two the old-fashioned way. He and Malone traded up for the WarnerMedia assets because they knew that Discovery Communications, alone, didn\xe2\x80\x99t have a prayer in the rapidly transforming media ecosystem. Now, though, he appears to have embraced the reality that bigger isn\xe2\x80\x99t always big enough and asset compositions matter. Did he underestimate a chill in the advertising market that would weigh down his linear business? Or the increasing rate of cord-cutting? Perhaps and maybe. But as the fourth quarter beckons, Zaz\xe2\x80\x99s list of headaches isn\xe2\x80\x99t limited to the tech insurgents who have turned media into their side hustle. He\xe2\x80\x99s also under pressure from the industry\xe2\x80\x99s incumbents, like Comcast and Paramount, with whom he now needs to cooperate more than ever.
FOUR STORIES WE\xe2\x80\x99RE TALKING ABOUT
Ichan\xe2\x80\x99s $20B Fumble
On billionaire investor\xc2\xa0Carl Icahn\xe2\x80\x99s dwindling fortune.
WILLIAM D. COHAN
LVMH Beauty Secrets
Peeking under the hood of LVMH\xe2\x80\x99s beauty empire.
RACHEL STRUGATZ & LAUREN SHERMAN
Aftershocks in Philly
Snap analysis of the\xc2\xa0Trump-Harris\xc2\xa0debate showdown.
PETER HAMBY, JOHN HEILEMANN & DYLAN BYERS
CNN\xe2\x80\x99s Financial Colonoscopy
Digging into a surge of defamation cases against mediacos.
ERIQ GARDNER
